does: This paper introduces RMGAP, a benchmark designed to evaluate how well reward models generalize across diverse user preferences. The authors create 1,097 test instances spanning Chat, Writing, Reasoning, and Safety domains, where each prompt has four responses with distinct linguistic profiles. Rather than assuming universal preferences, they construct tailored prompts by contrasting these candidates to create scenarios where different responses are appropriate for different user preferences. The benchmark explicitly measures whether reward models can correctly rank responses according to varied preference profiles rather than a single monolithic standard, directly addressing the gap between how current reward models are trained and how they will be deployed across heterogeneous user populations.
connects: This work extends the growing literature on evaluating reward models, which have become central to reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) alignment approaches for language models. It builds on prior efforts to benchmark reward model quality and generalization, but identifies and responds to a critical limitation: existing benchmarks typically assess performance against a single universal preference distribution. The paper connects to research on preference learning, human feedback quality, and the alignment problem in large language models, drawing on understanding that real-world users hold diverse, sometimes conflicting preferences. It also relates to work on distributional robustness in machine learning and the broader challenge of measuring model generalization beyond standard test sets.
enables: This benchmark supports future work in developing more robust reward models that genuinely align with diverse user populations rather than averaging over conflicting preferences. It enables researchers to identify failure modes in reward models stemming from preference heterogeneity and to design training procedures that improve cross-preference generalization. The work opens directions for exploring how reward models might better handle conflicting user preferences, whether through multi-objective optimization, preference uncertainty quantification, or hierarchical models that adapt to different user types. It also supports investigation into fundamental questions about whether single reward models can adequately represent diverse preferences or whether alternative alignment paradigms are needed, potentially influencing the design of future RLHF systems and human-AI collaboration frameworks.
